headline: Kirkwood Pulse • March 11, 2026
overview:
issue: Kirkwood is facing a tear-down crisis driven by high land costs, making it difficult to preserve affordable starter homes for young families.
key_points:
  -: Land cost accounts for 65% to 100% of property value, making it expensive to maintain small, affordable homes.
  -: Reducing demand for land is undesirable as high demand reflects Kirkwood's attractiveness and supports home value appreciation.
  -: Increasing land supply is challenging since land is finite, but building multi-family or multi-story homes can effectively increase housing supply per land unit.
  -: Small condo/apartment buildings and townhomes help but don't fully meet the traditional starter home ideal: a detached single-family home with a backyard.
  -: Another solution is subdividing land into smaller lots to lower individual land prices, making homeownership more attainable for modest-income families.
  -: Current zoning laws in Kirkwood impose minimum lot sizes (7,500 sqft to 1 acre), preventing lot splits and thus limiting the creation of smaller, affordable homes.
  -: Example: A 10,100 sqft lot at 511 W Rose Hill Ave could not be split due to the 7,500 sqft minimum, forcing redevelopment into a larger home instead of multiple modest homes.
conclusion: To revive starter homes, Kirkwood needs to reconsider zoning minimum lot sizes to allow smaller lot subdivisions and support affordable housing options while maintaining community desirability. event: Kirkwood City Council unanimously voted down a contract with Fybr for a parking technology solution on September 4th.
technology:
  provider: Fybr
  description: Sensors placed under parking spaces to provide real-time occupancy data, accessible via the Park Kirkwood app and used for parking enforcement.
  benefits:
  -: Real-time parking availability for visitors
  -: Automated alerts for parking limit violations
  -: More consistent enforcement compared to manual monitoring
background:
  original_contract:
  period: December 3, 2020 to December 2, 2021
  scope: 275 parking sensors installed at no cost to Kirkwood
  purpose: Proof of concept for Fybr and free product trial for Kirkwood
  miscommunication:
  error: City staff incorrectly reported the original contract as covering 69 spots at a cost of $14,973 instead of 275 spots at no cost.
  impact: This mistake led to confusion and misunderstandings during the new contract proposal process.
contract_renewal:
  status: Original contract expired December 2, 2021; city assumed ownership and maintenance of hardware.
  issues:
  -: City did not pay maintenance fees for year two.
  -: Communication gateways went offline due to power issues.
  -: Sensors' battery life diminished due to failed transmissions.
  actions:
  -: Fybr contacted city about sensor removal but received no response until 2025.
  -: City requested reactivation in January 2025.
  -: Fybr identified 69 sensors with sufficient battery life to relocate for $14,973.
conclusion: Council rejected the new contract amid confusion over costs and contract history, without a clear alternative plan for addressing downtown parking. project:
  name: Pitman Place
  location: 300 N. Kirkwood Road (former Commerce Bank site)
  type: 60-apartment mixed-use development
  developer: TriStar Properties with Stock & Associates
  approval_status: First reading approval granted by Kirkwood City Council on November 20th
  zoning_changes:
  allowed_residential_on_first_floor: True
  previous_restriction: Residential use on first floor banned
  reason_for_change: To allow preferred aesthetic design (Concept P) and more family-suitable larger apartments
  design_iterations:
  original:
  apartments:
  1_bed_1_bath: 27
  2_bed_2_bath: 27
  3_bed_2_bath: 6
  retail_space: baseline
  clubhouse_size: larger
  parking_spaces: baseline
  new_plan:
  apartments:
  1_bed: 23
  2_bed: 24
  3_bed: 13
  net_bedroom_increase: 11
  retail_space: a couple hundred square feet more than original
  clubhouse_size: slightly smaller
  parking_spaces: 6 fewer but still compliant
  historical_features:
  plaque: To be erected next to old Pitman School fence
  naming: Development named after historic Pitman School
context:
  zoning_code:
  original_requirement: Mixed-use with commercial space on ground floor along main streets
  goal: Active street front and walkable downtown
  criticism: Mandate can be counterproductive due to parking requirements and low foot traffic
  developer_incentive: Retail included where financially viable
  advocated_change: Allow residential on first floor to increase housing quantity, size, and affordability
  approval_process:
  timeline:
  -: Early October: Council delayed vote, developer withdrew proposal to restart process
  -: October 15th: Planning & Zoning hearing held
  -: November 20th: Council granted first reading approval
  public_and_council_feedback: Negative on original flat, bulky design
  design_response: Developer presented two new facade options with gabled designs
  preferred_design: Concept P with balconies and direct-entrance apartments on Adams facade

items.title: How To Revive the Starter Home
items.excerpt: I’ve long maintained that Kirkwood’s tear-down crisis is merely a symptom of how expensive our land has become. After you spend $500k to purchase the lot, spending another $200k to tear down the existing small home that sits on the lot and build a new bigger one seems like small potatoes. The Venn diagram of people who can afford to
items.ai_summary:
issue: Kirkwood is facing a tear-down crisis driven by high land costs, making it difficult to preserve affordable starter homes for young families.
key_points:
  -: Land cost accounts for 65% to 100% of property value, making it expensive to maintain small, affordable homes.
  -: Reducing demand for land is undesirable as high demand reflects Kirkwood's attractiveness and supports home value appreciation.
  -: Increasing land supply is challenging since land is finite, but building multi-family or multi-story homes can effectively increase housing supply per land unit.
  -: Small condo/apartment buildings and townhomes help but don't fully meet the traditional starter home ideal: a detached single-family home with a backyard.
  -: Another solution is subdividing land into smaller lots to lower individual land prices, making homeownership more attainable for modest-income families.
  -: Current zoning laws in Kirkwood impose minimum lot sizes (7,500 sqft to 1 acre), preventing lot splits and thus limiting the creation of smaller, affordable homes.
  -: Example: A 10,100 sqft lot at 511 W Rose Hill Ave could not be split due to the 7,500 sqft minimum, forcing redevelopment into a larger home instead of multiple modest homes.
conclusion: To revive starter homes, Kirkwood needs to reconsider zoning minimum lot sizes to allow smaller lot subdivisions and support affordable housing options while maintaining community desirability.

items.title: String of Errors Leads to Rejection of Parking Solution
items.excerpt: On September 4th, Kirkwood City Council unanimously voted down a contract with a local company, Fybr, for a technological solution that aimed to address the parking issue in Downtown Kirkwood. I was disappointed by that decision because I’ve long been a fan of Kirkwood’s previous adoption of the technology and especially its long-term potential. The more concerning aspect of the

items.title: Council Greenlights Pitman Place
items.excerpt: After winding itself through the approval process three times, the Council finally offered first reading approval to Pitman Place, the 60-apartment mixed-use project proposed for the former Commerce Bank site at 300 N. Kirkwood Road, at their November 20th meeting. The Kirkwood City Council was supposed to issue its verdict on the project in early October, but instead of approving
